data.title: ZetaChain Pauses Mainnet Cross-Chain Transactions After Attack On GatewayEVM Contract Sparks Fresh Bridge Security Fears
data.description:
Cross-chain activity on ZetaChain came to a sudden halt after a security incident hit its GatewayEVM contract, forcing the network to pause transfers while teams worked to contain the issue and assess potential exposure.

Cross-Chain Pause After Contract Attack Raised Alarm

ZetaChain confirmed that an attack targeted its GatewayEVM contract, prompting an immediate suspension of cross-chain transactions on its mainnet.

The team said the issue affected internal wallets only, not user deposits or funds.

In a public update on X, it stated:

“There was an attack against the ZetaChain GatewayEVM contract today that impacted the internal ZetaChain team wallets only. We’ve already blocked the attack vector so no more funds can be compromised and will be releasing a detailed post mortem after we have completed our investigation.”

As a precaution, the network paused cross-chain activity while engineers moved to patch the vulnerability and review the system.

There was an attack against the ZetaChain GatewayEVM contract today that impacted the internal ZetaChain team wallets only. We've already blocked the attack vector so no more funds can be compromised and will be releasing a detailed post mortem after we have completed our…

— ZetaChain 🟩 (@ZetaChain) April 27, 2026

Internal Wallets Hit But Were Users Exposed?

ZetaChain maintained that no user funds were impacted during the incident, despite early social media warnings suggesting a broader exploit.

The project added:

“As a precaution cross-chain transactions are currently paused on ZetaChain. Investigation is still ongoing and at this time no user funds were impacted by this attack.”

However, uncertainty spread quickly after a warning circulated on crypto channels claiming the bridge had been exploited, triggering calls for users to move assets.

The lack of an immediate full breakdown added to market concern, a common pattern in bridge-related incidents where information often lags behind speculation.

What Is The GatewayEVM Contract?

The compromised component, GatewayEVM, acts as the entry point for cross-chain activity between ZetaChain and external EVM-compatible networks such as Ethereum and Polygon.

It works alongside other core components including ERC20Custody, which holds tokens bridged into the network, and ZetaConnector, which manages token flows tied to ZETA across connected chains.

In simple terms, it is one of the main pathways used when assets or messages move between blockchains, making it a high-value target in interoperability networks.

🚨. @ZetaChain has been exploited. Based on initial analysis, the following outlines the root cause.

Root Cause
The core vulnerability lies in the call function of ZetaChain's GatewayZEVM contract, which lacks both access control and input validation. This allows any arbitrary… https://t.co/U63DKIfgDZpic.twitter.com/WbAHdiciRc

— SlowMist (@SlowMist_Team) April 28, 2026

Network Status Shows Systems Running But Transfers Frozen

Despite the incident, ZetaChain’s infrastructure remained largely operational.

Its status page showed all 11 mainnet components and testnet systems running at full uptime.

Only cross-chain transactions were disabled, with the incident listed as “identified” and “ongoing” for several hours while engineers confirmed the attack vector and prepared a fix.

At the time of reporting, the pause had lasted around nine hours since detection, with the team indicating a patch was being prepared.

How Much Was Lost In The Attack?

While ZetaChain has not confirmed exact figures, blockchain data cited by DefiLlama estimated losses of around $300,000.

NEW: @ZetaChain has reportedly been exploited, with at least $300K drained so far. pic.twitter.com/KcAaB1iOqD

— SolanaFloor (@SolanaFloor) April 27, 2026

The project has not publicly validated this figure and has said a detailed post-mortem will follow once the investigation concludes.

ZETA, the network’s token, holds a market capitalisation of roughly $73.3 million, with about 1.36 billion tokens in circulation out of a total supply of 2.1 billion.

Why Bridge Security Is Under Pressure Again

The incident lands during a turbulent period for decentralised finance, with April 2026 already recording one of the highest levels of crypto-related exploits in recent years.

Earlier this month, the Kelp DAO cross-chain exploit drained about $292 million, an incident later linked by multiple cybersecurity firms to the Lazarus Group.

Kelp DAO lost about $294M in a @LayerZero_Core bridge exploit.$ZRO dropped from $2 to $1.4, and a whale holding a $ZRO long on #HyperLiquid got partially liquidated, losing $2.88M.

The whale is still holding the position with a floating loss of over $750K. Overall, the whale… https://t.co/psD7U02563pic.twitter.com/1k7O4LJprw

— Onchain Lens (@OnchainLens) April 19, 2026

That attack alone triggered wider stress across DeFi, including heavy exposure risks on lending platforms such as Aave.

In response, an industry coalition named “DeFi United” began raising funds to cover losses, with contributions from major ecosystem players and founders helping push recovery efforts past $160 million.

defiunited.eth is now open for contributions. All contributions are going towards DeFi United relief efforts to restore rsETH and safe DeFi.https://t.co/gdkGe5pi0ehttps://t.co/lOlsI3SmVH

— Stani (@StaniKulechov) April 24, 2026

Across the month, more than $606 million has been lost across 12 separate incidents, according to DefiLlama data, with cross-chain bridges continuing to represent one of the most targeted attack surfaces in the sector.

Cross-Chain Design Remains A Persistent Weak Point

This latest ZetaChain incident adds to a long list of bridge and interoperability exploits in crypto history, including Ronin, Wormhole, and Nomad, each of which resulted in large-scale losses.

The repeated pattern has kept pressure on developers to strengthen verification systems, improve audit standards, and introduce safer withdrawal and transfer mechanisms for cross-chain liquidity.

ZetaChain has said it will publish a full post-mortem once its investigation is complete, while cross-chain services remain paused pending final confirmation of system integrity.

data.title: SEC Chairman: Embrace Digital Asset Innovation, End Enforcement-Driven Regulation
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

At the 2026 Bitcoin Conference in Las Vegas on Monday, U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) Chairman Paul Atkins stated that the SEC is committed to embracing digital asset innovation, ending its enforcement-oriented regulatory model, and working with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) to bring a clear regulatory framework to the U.S. cryptocurrency market.

In a conversation with Perianne Boring, founder and CEO of Digital Chamber, Paul Atkins described the SEC's previous attitude towards digital assets as a failure.

He stated that the SEC will abandon its past "ostrich policy" and "enforcement-style regulation" model, and instead embrace digital asset innovation, committed to strengthening cooperation with the CFTC and clarifying regulatory boundaries to retain relevant U.S. companies. He also explained the core of the security definition (focusing on project commitments rather than the token itself), mentioned the advancement of token classification guidelines, on-chain experiments, and related exemptions, emphasized the importance of market structure legislation for policy stability, and looked ahead to the development of blockchain instant settlement and tokenized stocks, with the core goal of promoting the U.S.'s global leadership in the digital asset field. The following is the full transcript of the dialogue, compiled by Jinse Finance (AI-assisted). Host: Chairman Atkins, thank you very much for coming to Las Vegas to speak with us. Paul Atkins: It's my pleasure. It's great to see you all.

Host: I think this is your first time—and I believe it's the first time the Chairman of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has spoken at a Bitcoin conference. At least that's what people say. Yes, we are honored to have you here.

Paul Atkins: Yes. Anyway, thank you very much.

Host: The relationship between the Bitcoin community and the SEC has always been quite interesting. You succeeded—what should we call him? Gary Gensler. He wasn't very open or accepting of digital assets. He certainly did his best to slow down the development of this innovation. You joined the Commission as part of the Trump administration. This was our first president to support Bitcoin and cryptocurrency, and you were really at the forefront of implementing this policy at the SEC. Before we dive into the details, could you briefly talk about your guiding principles as Chairman of the SEC and your views on digital assets? Paul Atkins: Okay. I think I should put it this way: This is a new day for the SEC. I think if you look at the last ten years or so, at the beginning, the SEC was like an ostrich burying its head in the sand, thinking maybe it would all just disappear on its own. Then came the phase of regulation through enforcement, and I think the SEC saying, “Oh, come talk to us,” was a bit insincere. You know, we have a simple form on our website called S-1. But that’s not an easy form to fill out. It takes a lot of lawyers and accountants to complete. It’s actually designed for IPOs, which is great for that kind of scenario, but it’s not very suitable for the digital asset space, especially tokens. So, we’re taking a new approach. We embrace innovation and genuinely want to help related businesses stay in the United States. We’re responding to the president’s challenge—he wants digital assets to be done in the United States, using American technology. So we’re really working towards that goal. You just saw Michael Selig from the Commodity Futures Trading Commission; he’s a great guy. He was in my office before, and I was very pleased that the President appointed him as Chairman of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission. He's done an excellent job, and we're working closely together. So, believe it or not, in Washington, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission and the Securities and Exchange Commission have never really collaborated historically. I'd rather compare it to two fortresses with no man's land in between, and the crossfire between the fortresses destroyed all those new products that could have been developed 40 years ago. So, we're going to change that now. We're going to really collaborate, set new benchmarks for the nation, and truly embrace change. Host: Yes, you've put it very well, the collaboration with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, and how important that is for regulatory clarity, innovation, market structure, and America's leadership in this industry. In my view, there has indeed been competition between the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission in the past, a struggle for jurisdiction. One agency would say it's our jurisdiction, the other would say it's ours, and then the private sector would wonder, who is the regulator? Who should we contact? This created enormous uncertainty and risk for businesses, leading many to either close down or leave the market, and those fortunate enough not to be pursued by law enforcement were left with no other option. That was the strategy at the time. Paul Atkins: Absolutely right. So the real problem is that the definition of a security is a very technical issue, and there isn't much guidance available. Therefore, the real breakthrough we're trying to push for largely stems from an old case from 1946 called *SEC v. Howe*. Mr. Howe had an orange grove in Florida, and many people wanted to participate and own a small stake. Basically, securities regulations mention various things like stocks, bonds, etc., but they introduce a concept called an "investment contract" without bothering to define it. So this case was essentially the U.S. Supreme Court trying to define it. Therefore, when we set out to apply this to digital assets, we found that in Mr. Howe's case, the investment contract wasn't the oranges themselves—here equivalent to tokens, Bitcoin, or anything else—but rather Mr. Howe's commitment to his investors and the entire ecosystem that investors expected. So we've now applied this to the entire digital asset space, and through our partnership with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, we released a joint statement in which we explained what tokenized securities are. So that was a major breakthrough. We released that statement earlier this year, and now we're moving on to other exciting things, like truly allowing businesses to experiment on-chain, build their tokenized securities, and trade them on-chain within the U.S. We plan to release an exemption for innovation in the coming weeks. Additionally, it will allow financing through our on-chain token sales. These things are in the works and will be rolling out soon. But we know that just a few minutes ago, a regulation was discussed here, the Clarity Act, which is currently under consideration in Congress. We really need Congress to regulate this space. We are ready, willing, and able to interpret their regulations and translate them into rules that people can rely on and pursue their innovative ideas. Again, it's within the U.S., they don't have to go overseas. That's the core idea that's really important here.

Host: Yes, there's a lot to elaborate on. So this isn't just about providing regulatory clarity and helping companies understand when the SEC's jurisdiction begins and ends, and where the Commodity Futures Trading Commission's jurisdiction lies. This has always been one of the most complex and challenging issues for everyone. But we need to take more than just steps toward regulatory clarity; we need to be able to bring more financial applications on-chain. There's a lot to cover. Perhaps we could start with token classification guidance to help clarify which agency has jurisdiction over different types of tokens—digital securities, digital commodities, digital collectibles, and so on. Regarding digital commodities, in your token classification guidance—you actually announced this a few weeks ago at my Washington Blockchain Summit—thank you. That was a very exciting announcement, and something we've been talking about for a long time: establishing a classification system. The SEC has clearly listed some tokens that are considered digital commodities. We've seen the market react to this, and now these tokens are trading at a premium, especially in Asian markets. So, this raises another question: what if the token I'm interested in isn't on that list, but we believe it meets all other criteria and therefore is still a digital commodity? What are your comments on how market participants can gain more clarity and how they should interpret this list? Paul Atkins: Yes. This is a very important question. So, in developing this explanatory document, we gave many examples, which are just illustrative. This doesn't mean it's a fixed list. But we talked about the principles behind it, tracing back to the Howey Test, which isn't about the orange itself, but about the commitments surrounding it. So, what's really important is that things can start with those commitments, within that framework, but then those commitments can be fulfilled, or they can disappear and become unenforceable. That's the core work we've done about the nature of securities, and that's the approach we've taken. So what we're trying to do is exclude these digital goods, digital instruments, digital collectibles, and of course, stablecoins. These are clearly not securities. Stablecoins, because Congress has passed and the President has signed the Genius Act, is a big step forward. For the first time, the U.S. has recognized an entire class of digital assets and provided a framework for things like being able to trade them. So this is very important. Therefore, we focus on tokenized securities. We're trying to define them as not belonging to the other four categories. That's the key. It's a principle-based approach. So, if people have questions, we welcome them to seek clarification, but we hope that this document we've released will make things clearer. Obviously, things can always be improved and further explained.

Host: Okay. Let's talk about the Market Structures Act. Senator Cynthia Lummis was just here giving us a timeline of the bill's passage, and she expects progress in May, a Senate vote in June, and hopefully it will be on the president's desk shortly after passing the House. I mean, a lot of things have to happen. I used to be a congressional staffer, and I understand the legislative path; a lot of things have to be aligned to get it done. Of course, we hope this will happen, but it's not a done deal. So, in case the Market Structures Act doesn't pass, I know a lot of people are worried, especially those who have been through several cycles: What will happen after the Trump administration? What if the new administration is as hostile to cryptocurrencies as the Biden administration, and we have a new SEC chairman like Gary Gensler who wants to shut it down? That's why a lot of people think the Market Structures Act is so important for making all the work of this administration resilient to future changes. So, could you talk about this concern? What are you doing to mitigate this in case the Market Structures Act doesn't pass? How much of a reaction should people reasonably expect if the bill doesn't pass? Paul Atkins: Good question. I think you all realize that elections do have consequences, and potentially huge ones. What I mean here is, who could have imagined that 10 years ago, the U.S. government—including us, the SEC, banking regulators, and Congress—would have made such a complete turn, almost a 180-degree turn? So, that's wonderful. The SEC has a lot of room to maneuver under the regulations, we can be flexible, but we are bound by existing powers that, while revised over the years, are essentially still the framework of the 1930s. So, that's why having a regulation that protects future developments from adverse effects, and then we can utilize the new powers, and the flexibility that the bill provides. We can work with the SEC to harmonize and clarify definitions, and build upon that. But to reiterate, nothing provides a better guarantee for the future than codified law, coupled with sound judicial advice, and the mechanisms of the entire court system that inscribe the provisions of codified law in stone. So all of this is extremely important, but we are focused on streamlining processes, improving efficiency, and helping innovators innovate so they can do it confidently, rather than being killed by those who jealously cling to existing ways of doing things. However, we want to ensure we are at the forefront of innovation in the United States. Host: Speaking of being at the forefront of innovation, one area in our market that is very well positioned to benefit from the power of blockchain is tokenized stock, which you mentioned earlier. The committee is in a very important position to facilitate this innovation. One challenge I see is that tokenized stock involves many stakeholders, many participants, and many steps between transaction execution and settlement, with intermediaries taking fees along the way. Could you talk about all the stakeholders in this process? Can we achieve the full benefits of blockchain (i.e., instant settlement) while also managing all these different participants who either have to participate or whose business models may be altered or eliminated as a result? Paul Atkins: Yes. That's a great question. I know we'll probably be closing up soon. Host: Yes, time flies. Paul Atkins: For me, blockchain—distributed ledger technology—is, in my opinion, the most exciting aspect of all this. I'm a little hesitant to say this at a Bitcoin conference, but you know, I'm basically agnostic about specific tokens themselves. Let the market decide what's best. But being able to achieve what we call T+0, instant settlement for many instruments—maybe not for everything—but instant delivery to payment, receipt to payment, I think is the most exciting because it reduces risk in our financial system, because every second between transaction time and clearing settlement is a risk borne by investors and both parties. In short, we're working hard to promote and achieve this within the United States. There are many existing players, including current exchanges, etc., but we also want to open it up to them. This is also a way for them to reduce their own market risk. In addition, there are many good ideas from other diverse groups, from the native crypto and native blockchain communities. We hope that all these different flowers can bloom, which will solidify the United States' position as a market for ideas and financial innovation, which is better for the world, for consumers, for investors, and for our overall economy. I think that's what we're trying to do.

Host: I think that's a good closing. Chairman Atkins, thank you very much.

Paul Atkins: Thank you for speaking with me.

Host: It's a pleasure to see you again.

Paul Atkins: Thank you everyone.

data.title: Western Union Eyes May Launch for USDPT Stablecoin On Solana To Replace SWIFT Network
data.description:
Western Union has just announced that it will be launching its USDPT stable in May, marking a decisive shift from experimentation to full-scale integration of digital assets across its global payments infrastructure.

WESTERN UNION SOLANA-BASED STABLECOIN TO LAUNCH NEXT MONTH

Global financial giant Western Union is preparing to launch its own US dollar-backed stablecoin, USDPT, on Solana $SOL, as early as next month, CEO Devin McGranahan confirmed during the company's first-quarter earnings… pic.twitter.com/y6BL7Zuk7S

— BSCN (@BSCNews) April 27, 2026

CEO Devin McGranahan signaled urgency behind the move, noting

"Over the last few months, we've crossed an important threshold. It is no longer a question of if Western Union will be active in digital assets, it is now how fast we can scale."

USDPT Becomes the Core Settlement Layer

At the center of that strategy is USDPT, a US dollar-backed stablecoin built on Solana and issued by Anchorage Digital. Now in its final stages of readiness, the token is designed to function as a programmable settlement asset across Western Union’s ecosystem.

Rather than operating as a standalone digital token, USDPT is being positioned as a native payment rail that enables faster cross-border transfers, real-time settlement, and treasury use cases across partner banks and financial institutions.

Exchange partners will support access, liquidity, and distribution, ensuring the stablecoin can move efficiently between crypto-native environments and traditional financial systems.

Crucially, the rollout of USDPT is not happening in isolation—it is being built on top of Western Union’s Digital Asset Network (DAN), which acts as the connective infrastructure powering the entire system.

Western Union is building something far bigger than most people realise.

Malcolm Clarke, Head of Digital Assets at @WesternUnion, explained how their Digital Asset Network could let wallets exit to cash or bank rails through trusted infrastructure at real-world scale.

This is… pic.twitter.com/22exL7tG89

— ALLINCRYPTO (@RealAllinCrypto) April 15, 2026

DAN is designed to bridge blockchain-based assets with Western Union’s vast network of more than 360,000 global cash access points. This effectively addresses one of crypto’s most persistent limitations: the ability to convert digital value into usable, real-world money at scale.

Through this network, users can move stablecoins like USDPT across borders and seamlessly convert digital dollars into local currency, whether for cash pickup or deposit within Western Union’s retail ecosystem.

With tens of millions of crypto wallets expected to connect via ecosystem partners, DAN transforms USDPT from a simple stablecoin into a globally interoperable payment layer. It links decentralized financial infrastructure with traditional remittance rails, allowing value to flow more freely between the two systems.

Stablecoin Card Completes the Ecosystem

To further extend its reach, Western Union is also preparing to launch a US dollar-denominated stablecoin card later this year. The product will allow users to hold and spend stablecoins directly at merchants, bringing digital assets into everyday transactions.

Taken together, USDPT, DAN, and the upcoming stablecoin card form a fully integrated ecosystem that spans the entire lifecycle of digital money—from acquiring stablecoins, to transferring and settling value, to converting and spending funds in the real world.

🔥HUGE: WESTERN UNION TO LAUNCH SOLANA-BASED STABLECOIN NEXT MONTH

The firm plans to launch USD-backed stablecoin USDPT, for onchain settlement as an alternative to SWIFT.

It also plans to launch “Stable Card” for global consumer spending and stablecoin access across markets. pic.twitter.com/McpJZJj5Wa

— Coin Bureau (@coinbureau) April 27, 2026

This positions Western Union as more than a remittance provider, evolving it into a hybrid crypto-financial platform capable of operating across both blockchain and traditional payment infrastructures.

Currently, US dollar-denominated stablecoins account for the lion's share of the $320 billion stablecoin market capitalization.

🔥 LATEST: Tiger Research reports Tether and USDC control over 85% of the stablecoin market as total market cap reaches around $300 billion. pic.twitter.com/P511DRzjDx

— Cointelegraph (@Cointelegraph) April 23, 2026

USDPT will enter a competitive battleground currently dominated by Tether (USDT) and USD Coin (USDC). However, Western Union’s extensive physical distribution network and regulatory footprint could give it a unique advantage, particularly in regions where access to cash remains essential.

From Experimentation to Core Infrastructure

McGranahan emphasized that Western Union’s long-term strategy is not limited to launching new products, but rather embedding digital assets deeply into its operational foundation.

“The focus ahead is scaling, expanding adoption, increasing velocity, and integrating digital assets into our core money movement platform.”

With USDPT as its foundation and DAN as its delivery engine, Western Union is positioning itself to play a central role in the next phase of stablecoin-driven global payments.

data.title: US Government Joins xAI In Challenging Colorado AI Law As Court Case And Enforcement Are Temporarily Put On Hold
data.description:
Colorado’s legal clash with Elon Musk’s AI firm xAI has been paused for now after both sides agreed to temporarily suspend court proceedings while a broader policy and constitutional battle over artificial intelligence rules unfolds in the United States.

Case Put On Hold As Both Sides Agree To Pause Proceedings

Court filings submitted jointly by xAI and Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser asked a federal judge to cancel a scheduled June 16 hearing and freeze all current deadlines linked to the lawsuit.

The agreement also temporarily halts enforcement of Senate Bill 24-205, including any replacement legislation emerging from the current state review process.

Under the arrangement, the attorney general will not take enforcement action or open investigations against xAI until at least 14 days after the court rules on the company’s planned request for a preliminary injunction.

Looks like Colorado’s AI law SB24-205 is on pause for now, and so is xAI’s lawsuit challenging it.

On Friday, the parties jointly agreed to suspend all case deadlines while Colorado works through potential changes to the law. Enforcement is also on hold during this process.

A… https://t.co/zxLpMGrBU7pic.twitter.com/9wJ00OZY67

— FIRE (@TheFIREorg) April 27, 2026

xAI has committed to filing that injunction motion within 28 days after Colorado finalises updated rules or any revised version of the law.

The move comes as Colorado lawmakers consider amendments to the AI framework, while the court case remains active in the background.

Does Colorado’s AI Law Force Ideological Output?

xAI originally filed its lawsuit on 9 April in Denver federal court, seeking to block the law before it comes into force.

The company argues that SB24-205 would interfere with how AI systems like its chatbot Grok generate responses, effectively forcing them to reflect state-approved interpretations of fairness and discrimination.

xAI’s attorneys wrote in the complaint,

“SB24-205 is decidedly not an anti-discrimination law. It is instead an effort to embed the State’s preferred views into the very fabric of AI systems.”

The company also claims the law breaches First Amendment protections by compelling speech, is too vague to enforce fairly, and could apply unevenly to different AI developers.

It further argues the statute attempts to regulate conduct beyond Colorado’s borders.

At the heart of the dispute is whether the state can require AI systems to actively prevent what it defines as “algorithmic discrimination” without crossing into control over the content and outputs of generative models.

This is the most important AI lawsuit in America right now and almost nobody understands why.

Colorado's SB24-205 requires AI companies to document and mitigate algorithmic discrimination risks when their models are used in employment, housing, healthcare, and lending decisions.… https://t.co/DZcvJum6XA

— Aakash Gupta (@aakashgupta) April 10, 2026

US Justice Department Steps In Supporting xAI

The legal fight escalated further when the US Department of Justice moved to intervene in support of xAI, marking the federal government’s first direct challenge to a state AI law under the current administration.

Officials from the DOJ’s Civil Rights and Civil Divisions argue that the Colorado law raises constitutional concerns, claiming it pressures AI companies to embed ideological preferences into their systems.

Assistant Attorney General Harmeet K. Dhillon said,

“Laws that require AI companies to infect their products with woke DEI ideology are illegal.”

Justice Department Intervenes in xAI lawsuit Challenging Colorado’s ‘Algorithmic Discrimination’ Law

“Laws that require AI companies to infect their products with woke DEI ideology are illegal,” said @AAGDhillon. “The Justice Department will not stand on the sidelines while… pic.twitter.com/sSZkPIIeek

— DOJ Civil Rights Division (@CivilRights) April 24, 2026

Assistant Attorney General Brett A. Shumate added that such rules risk distorting innovation, stating that they could push developers towards “ideological bias” instead of neutral outputs and threaten US leadership in the global AI sector.

The DOJ also argued that the statute could violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment by treating different types of discrimination inconsistently.

What Colorado’s AI Act Requires From Developers

Senate Bill 24-205, also known as the Colorado AI Act or Consumer Protections for Artificial Intelligence, is set to take effect on 30 June.

It applies to “high-risk” AI systems used in decisions such as hiring, housing, healthcare, insurance and education.

The law requires developers to exercise “reasonable care” to prevent “algorithmic discrimination”, defined as unfair outcomes affecting protected groups including race, religion, sex, disability and national origin.

Today, @CivilRights & @DOJCivil intervened in a lawsuit to prevent Colorado from requiring AI companies to alter their algorithms & advance CO’s woke DEI goals.

It’s illegal under the 14th Amendment to discriminate based on race, sex, & other protected classes—see you in court! pic.twitter.com/3MpJyn14Gn

— AAGHarmeetDhillon (@AAGDhillon) April 24, 2026

Companies must carry out risk assessments, disclose system usage, monitor bias, and report suspected discrimination to the state attorney general within 90 days.

Violations can carry penalties of up to 20,000 US dollars each.

Growing Fault Lines Over Who Should Regulate AI

Colorado’s case is unfolding alongside wider political and legal tensions over how artificial intelligence should be governed in the US.

Several states, including California and New York, are advancing their own AI frameworks, while federal officials have pushed for a more unified national approach.

With xAI, the DOJ, and Colorado all now engaged in the dispute, the case has become a focal point in the broader debate over how far governments can go in regulating AI systems without crossing constitutional boundaries.

data.title: Meta's acquisition of Manus rejected: The end and warning of a $2 billion deal
data.description:
Author: Gu Lingyu

On April 27, the Office of the Foreign Investment Security Review Mechanism (National Development and Reform Commission) issued a brief announcement: In accordance with laws and regulations, a decision was made to prohibit foreign investment in the Manus project, requiring the parties involved to withdraw the acquisition transaction.

The announcement consisted of only one sentence.

This transaction, valued at $2-3 billion and dubbed the third largest M&A deal in Meta history, ended in this manner less than four months after its official announcement. This is also the first time China has formally halted a large-scale cross-border M&A in the AI ​​field through the Foreign Investment Security Review Mechanism.

Behind this outcome lies the complete story of a Chinese AI startup attempting to navigate the regulatory gap between China and the US, ultimately failing on both fronts. Several lawyers and industry insiders stated that the Manus incident serves as a warning for such projects: companies must clarify their identity as early as possible. One lawyer stated that Manus is not the only company facing compliance scrutiny; "actively cooperating with regulators and building trust with regulatory bodies is the best solution." The Butterfly Effect in Ten Months On March 6, 2025, Beijing Butterfly Effect Technology Co., Ltd. launched Manus, claiming it to be "the world's first general-purpose AI agent." The product adopted an invitation-only beta testing method, with invitation codes once fetching tens of thousands of yuan. In late April, Butterfly Effect secured $75 million in Series B funding led by US venture capital firm Benchmark, valuing the company at nearly $500 million post-money. Previously, ZhenFund, Sequoia China, and other institutions had already invested in earlier rounds. Shortly after the funding announcement, a turning point arrived. In July 2025, Manus co-founder Zhang Tao revealed in a keynote speech at a conference in Singapore that the company headquarters had moved to Singapore. That same month, Manus laid off staff in its domestic operations, retaining only about 40 of its 120 employees. Core technical personnel were relocated to the Singapore headquarters, and domestic social media accounts were deleted, while the official website blocked Chinese IP addresses. This series of actions was widely seen as paving the way for future developments by stripping away its "Chinese identity" through a third-country entity. On December 30, 2025, Meta announced its acquisition of Butterfly Effect, the parent company of Manus. Negotiations took only a little over ten days, and founder Xiao Hong would become Meta's vice president. Manus, with annualized revenue exceeding $125 million, became the most watched sale in China's AI startup circle that year. ZhenFund, as a follow-on investor in all four rounds of financing for Manus, was originally poised to be the biggest financial beneficiary of the deal. However, this transaction faced compliance concerns from the outset. On January 8, 2026, He Yadong, spokesperson for the Ministry of Commerce, stated at a regular press conference, in response to related questions, that enterprises engaging in overseas investment, technology export, data export, cross-border mergers and acquisitions must comply with Chinese laws and regulations and fulfill statutory procedures. The Ministry of Commerce, together with relevant departments, will conduct an assessment and investigation into the consistency of this acquisition with laws and regulations related to export controls, technology import and export, and overseas investment. The ban took effect on April 27. As of press time, Manus has not yet issued a response. The red line touches on national security concerns. The legal tool ultimately cited by the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) is the "Measures for Security Review of Foreign Investment," not the export control rules previously expected. This mechanism, jointly led by the NDRC and the Ministry of Commerce, is a cross-departmental joint review mechanism. Once a transaction touches on national security concerns, it can be included in the review process. The review result can be approval, conditional approval, or prohibition with a requirement for revocation. At the time of the transaction, Manus's domestic team, local products, and domestic patents were almost completely wiped out; only the founding team members remained Chinese citizens. Caixin quoted Jia Shen, a senior consultant at Zhong Lun Law Firm, as saying that this practice is a typical example of extraterritorial jurisdiction. Both parties to the transaction are foreign entities, and the measures serve more as a deterrent, signaling to the market that similar transactions will not be approved in the future. Previously, several lawyers pointed out in media interviews that the Manus case violated several red lines of Chinese law. The first is technology export control. "Although China has regulations restricting technology exports in the past, they haven't been widely enforced. Manus will serve as a typical case, demonstrating that technology developed in China, or at least meeting certain conditions, cannot be easily transferred overseas. This transfer isn't just about acquisitions; if it involves an overseas subsidiary or affiliated company, licensing or transferring technology could face compliance issues under Chinese law," an unnamed lawyer told us. The second point is data security. Manus products use a large amount of data from within China during training. Several lawyers pointed out that if this data contains personal information of Chinese residents, Manus must undergo a rigorous data export security assessment before transferring its products and technology to overseas companies. The third point is compliance with foreign mergers and acquisitions. The structure of this transaction is that the US company Meta acquired the Singaporean company Butterfly Effect, but Butterfly Effect's core technology was developed in China by a Chinese team, and its main source of revenue is also in China. The "Measures for Security Review of Foreign Investment" clearly stipulates that foreign investment involving key technologies, important information technologies, and internet products and services must undergo security review. For transactions that are not reported, regulatory authorities can initiate investigations afterward. Xia Bikang, a partner at Yingli Law Firm, told First Financial Daily that when Manus moved its registration to Singapore, it was very likely that it had already crossed the red line of technology export controls, and Meta's huge acquisition was merely a "magnifying glass" that put this hidden risk under the spotlight. The deeper logic behind the regulatory action lies in correcting policy direction—faced with high-profile public opinion, regulators also need to clarify their stance and policy direction to avoid creating a tacit incentive effect for potential inappropriate behavior. The aforementioned lawyer, who wished to remain anonymous, stated that the trajectory of the Manus case is highly correlated with the company's own choices. He believes that the Manus team made a fundamental mistake in its direction. "After they gained popularity within their small circle in February 2025, while I wouldn't say they actively facilitated it, they at least enjoyed being portrayed as 'the next DeepSeek,' a representative of Chinese companies. However, in the blink of an eye, in July 2025, simply because they received inquiries and investigations from the U.S. Treasury Department—which weren't even legally binding documents—they chose the most drastic measure to circumvent Reverse CFIUS scrutiny: moving the entire company to Singapore." His assessment is that the place of registration was not the core of their mistake. "Don't think like a test-taker. Regulation in any country isn't like a standardized exam; it's based on the specific circumstances of each company at different times." Regarding the $2 billion refund: According to Article 12 of the "Measures for the Security Review of Foreign Investment," after the state makes a decision to prohibit investment, the core requirement is to restore the investment to its pre-investment state within a specified period to eliminate any impact on national security. According to AIPress, canceling the transaction involves multiple levels of operations. At the equity level, all parties need to sign a written termination agreement to cancel the acquisition and terminate all supporting documents. If Meta has completed the equity transfer, it needs to transfer all its Manus shares back to the original shareholders or domestic entities and complete the business registration and overseas entity change registration. On the financial front, Meta is required to fully refund the approximately $2 billion already paid. Upon receiving the funds, the original shareholders must, in accordance with regulatory requirements, complete the foreign exchange repatriation and report to the foreign exchange regulatory authorities. The foreign exchange management department will verify the entire fund flow to prevent capital flight under the guise of terminating the transaction. On the data and technology front, Meta is required to delete all acquired Manus user data, training data, and business data from within China, issue a deletion certificate, and accept verification. Manus is required to restore local data storage, terminate all technology licenses and code transfers to Meta, and reclaim control of its core AI technology and algorithm models. If the requirements are not met, regulatory authorities may impose penalties such as fines, restrictions on domestic business operations, and prohibitions on foreign investment activities by relevant entities. The Reconstruction of the Logic of Going Global

The impact of the Manus case has extended beyond this single company.

Previously, it was predicted that there might be multiple paths for this transaction, including partial approval, conditional approval, or the transaction proceeding but with all proceeds being fined. Ultimately, the regulatory authorities chose the most thorough approach: prohibiting investment and demanding its cancellation.

It is widely believed that this in itself is a signal.Some argue that this is a clear statement of China's sovereign jurisdiction in the field of AI.

An investor close to Manus told us that acquisitions of AI companies by domestic and international tech giants are a crucial exit strategy for investors, but this path is now fraught with policy risks, raising questions about future exit strategies. However, he believes that, in terms of the industry's substance, the Manus incident has a limited impact on the valuation logic of AI overseas expansion projects. Valuation logic is dynamic and related to the level of the primary market. The explosive popularity of Manus last year was, to some extent, a man-made hype event, but relatively speaking, investors are more rational than public opinion. Ultimately, the impact of this event depends on the company's core intrinsic value. He believes that the Manus incident will likely accelerate the retention of AI talent in China, not out of necessity, but based on a choice of the domestic entrepreneurial environment. "For top talent, mobility doesn't exist. If you want to start a business, it must belong to a specific time and space. These new generation of entrepreneurs in China, as the smartest group, should have sufficient ability to interpret these things." In China, the AI ​​Agent track has not waited for Manus in the past period. Back in July 2025, when Manus "ran away" with its business, AutoGLM had already positioned itself to compete with Manus's core scenarios in terms of product form. This year, with the rise of the "lobster craze" and the collective involvement of various companies in the intelligent agent market, Manus's uniqueness has been further diluted—a number of major companies and emerging products, such as Lunar Dark Side, DeepSeek, and Tencent WorkBuddy, have already filled the ecosystem niche for domestic agents. Regarding the architectural choices for AI companies going global in the future, the aforementioned anonymous lawyer suggests that companies must clearly define their identity as early as possible. “If the ‘R&D in China, sales overseas’ model is indeed the way to go, then companies may have to become companies that only do sales overseas, without possessing any core technologies, while the core technologies remain in China. This would eliminate the so-called technology export or transfer issues. As long as sales are strong enough, or if they primarily use open-source models, overseas financing and even future IPOs may not present substantial problems. However, if they want to package R&D and sales into a story with a higher valuation, they need to truly clarify whether they are a Chinese or American company, because this involves China's technology export controls,” he stated. He believes the Manus incident may drive the refinement of technology export regulations. "From a legal perspective, we expect that after the Manus case, there will be more detailed rules and precedents regarding what can and cannot be done in technology exports. This will provide stronger reference for similar transactions in the future. Just like after the Didi incident, the China Securities Regulatory Commission's overseas listing filing became a substantive review process. The Manus case may be similar—in the past, most companies didn't bother with technology export declarations, but after the Manus case, everyone will feel that this is very important and must be done." According to his understanding, Manus is not the only company facing similar compliance reviews. He stated, "Actively cooperating with regulators and building trust with regulatory authorities is the best solution."

data.title: Manus Acquisition Halted: A Comparison of Reviews of Cutting-Edge Technology Acquisitions in China and the US
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

I. The Background of the Manus Acquisition

In March 2025, a general-purpose AI agent product called Manus emerged. Developed in China by its founder Xiao Hong and his team, this product ignited the global market with its ability to "think autonomously, plan, and execute complex tasks end-to-end," gaining widespread attention upon launch and quickly exceeding $125 million in annualized revenue. Within just nine months, Manus became a phenomenal star product in China's AI field and is considered one of the representative works of China's AI "leapfrog development."

... However, after its stunning debut, Manus quickly launched a swift and covert strategic maneuver. In June 2025, the company moved its headquarters from China to Singapore, and its operating entity was changed to the Singapore-based company Butterfly Effect Pte, which is wholly owned by its Cayman Islands parent company. Subsequently, the Chinese team underwent significant layoffs, with only about 40 core technical personnel remaining out of 120 employees and relocating to Singapore. Domestic social media accounts were deleted, and the official website blocked access from Chinese IP addresses. On December 30th of the same year, US tech giant Meta announced its acquisition of Butterfly Effect, the parent company of Manus, for approximately $2 billion. Founder Xiao Hong was scheduled to become Meta's vice president. This transaction briefly made it the third largest acquisition in Meta's history, second only to WhatsApp's $19 billion acquisition in 2014. Following Meta's official announcement of the acquisition, Chinese regulatory authorities quickly intervened. On January 8th, 2026, He Yadong, spokesperson for the Ministry of Commerce, publicly stated for the first time at a regular press conference that the Ministry would work with relevant departments to conduct an assessment and investigation into the consistency of Meta's acquisition of Manus with relevant laws and regulations concerning export controls, technology import and export, and foreign investment. Subsequently, the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) summoned senior executives from both parties, pointing out the risks of technology transfer and data security. Ultimately, on April 27, 2026, the Office of the Foreign Investment Security Review Mechanism (NDRC) issued a decision prohibiting investment in Meta's acquisition of Manus, requiring the parties to withdraw the acquisition. This was the first publicly halted foreign acquisition in the AI ​​field since the implementation of the "Measures for Foreign Investment Security Review" in 2020, and also the strictest review conclusion under the framework of these measures. The core controversy of the Manus case lies in the fact that the core technology was developed domestically by a Chinese team, but through a chain of "domestic R&D → overseas shell company → foreign acquisition," the company attempted to transfer control of the technology to a foreign company without legally declaring a foreign investment security review. The regulatory authorities have determined that their real concern is "when, how, and what will be transferred out"—changes in the place of registration cannot escape the jurisdiction of Chinese law. II. Basis for Review: Differences between the Chinese and American Legal Systems China's legal system. China's foreign investment security review system is based on the Foreign Investment Law, establishing a management model of "pre-establishment national treatment plus a negative list." The Measures for Foreign Investment Security Review, issued in 2020 and implemented in 2021, is the core implementing document. According to these measures, the scope of review covers key areas such as military industry, key technologies and infrastructure, important information technology and internet products and services, and important financial services. It is worth emphasizing that China's review mechanism adopts a penetrating review principle. As demonstrated in the Manus case, regulatory authorities are not fixated on whether the "investor entity is still formally a Chinese company," but rather pursue the "birthplace and growth path" of the technology—as long as the core R&D results are completed within China, even if the place of registration is changed overseas, it may still be included in China's regulatory scope. The US legal system. The US foreign investment security review system is based on Section 721 of the Defense Production Act of 1950, and its current framework has been formed after significant expansion by the Foreign Investment Risk Review Modernization Act (FIRRMA). FIRRMA is the most significant expansion of foreign investment review authority in the United States in 40 years, greatly broadening the scope of review. Before FIRRMA, CFIUS (Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States) primarily reviewed transactions involving a "transfer of control." After FIRRMA, even non-controlling investments that do not constitute a transfer of control fall under its review scope. CFIUS is an interagency committee led by the Treasury Department, with members including heads of key departments such as the Department of Defense, the Department of State, the Department of Commerce, the Department of Homeland Security, and the Department of Justice. The difference lies in the institutionalized differentiated arrangements for the "country of origin" in the US review system. In 2025, the Trump administration launched the "America First Investment Policy," which, on the one hand, restricts foreign investment in key technologies, infrastructure, and personal data, and on the other hand, opens a "fast track" pilot program for allied capital. III. Review Procedures: Proactive Declaration and Reactive Tracking China's review procedures. China's foreign investment security review process consists of three stages: The first stage is the preliminary review, which decides whether to initiate a security review within 15 working days of receiving compliant application materials; the second stage is the general review, completed within 30 working days of initiation; if it is believed to affect or potentially affect national security, a third stage special review is initiated, completed within 60 working days of initiation, which may be further extended if necessary. Review conclusions are categorized into three types: approved, conditionally approved, and investment prohibited. The Manus case's review process was unique—it was a post-event review of a "failed to report" transaction. The parties to the transaction failed to proactively report it, but regulatory authorities noticed this through publicly available information, initiated an investigation according to law, and completed the full set of procedures, ultimately making a prohibition decision. The US review process. CFIUS's review also consists of two phases: preliminary review and investigation. The first phase review period is 45 days. If a national security risk is found, a second 45-day investigation period begins. If the issue remains unresolved, the president can be advised to intervene in the transaction, and the president has 15 days to make a final decision. For certain types of transactions, if the key technology produced by the US target company requires an export license to the buyer's home country, the parties must submit a mandatory declaration to CFIUS. A key difference between CFIUS and China's review mechanisms lies in their retrospective capabilities. CFIUS not only reviews incomplete transactions but also conducts ex post review of completed transactions—the court even granted CFIUS an ex post divestiture order for the HieFo-Emcore transaction, even though the acquisition was completed nearly two years ago. Furthermore, CFIUS has a dedicated "Unreported Transactions Team" that proactively identifies transactions that may fall under its jurisdiction but for which no reporting has been received from the parties involved. IV. Review Focus: Data, Technology, and National Security The core concerns of China's review. China's review focuses on three key areas. First, there was the loss of key core technologies. In the Manus case, whether Manus's core AI technology fell under the control points of "information processing technology" in the "Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited and Restricted from Export in China" was one of the key assessments in the review. Second, there was the issue of data security when transferring data abroad. During its training and operation in China, Manus collected a large amount of data on Chinese users, raising compliance issues regarding the transfer of personal information abroad. Third, there was the issue of changes in control that were more substantial than formal. Regulatory authorities penetrated Manus's offshore structure to directly examine "when, how, and from whom the technology was transferred." The core concerns of the U.S. review. The core of CFIUS review is "risk-based analysis," which considers the threats posed by foreign investors, vulnerabilities of the target U.S. company, and the potential consequences of the transaction being exploited. Specifically, CFIUS focuses on three main dimensions: First, technological sensitivity, examining whether the target company involves key technologies covered by export control laws, such as semiconductors, AI, and quantum computing; second, data risk, examining whether the transaction would result in a large influx of sensitive personal data into the hands of foreign adversaries; and third, country of origin differences, applying stricter differentiated review standards to investments from countries such as China. Furthermore, CFIUS reviews are increasingly intertwined with and aligned with U.S. domestic industrial policies—the core controversy surrounding Nippon Steel's acquisition of U.S. Steel has extended beyond traditional national security concerns to include labor rights and industrial competition. V. Penalties: Prohibition and Divestiture China's penalty framework. According to the "Measures for Security Review of Foreign Investment," prohibition of investment is the most severe review conclusion—the measures explicitly stipulate that "investment shall not be carried out in areas where investment is prohibited." If the party concerned refuses to comply, the working mechanism office may order the disposal of equity or assets within a specified period and include its poor credit record in the relevant national credit information system for joint disciplinary action. The enforcement requirements in the Manus case are extremely specific. At the equity level, if Meta has completed the equity transfer, it must transfer all of its Manus shares back to the original shareholders or domestic entities, and complete the business registration and overseas entity change registration. At the financial level, Meta must fully refund the approximately US$2 billion already paid. The foreign exchange management department will verify the fund flow throughout the process to prevent capital flight under the guise of terminating the transaction. At the data and technology level, Meta must delete all acquired domestic data, Manus must restore local data storage, terminate all technology licenses and code transfers to Meta, and all Meta-assigned management and technical personnel must withdraw. The working mechanism office will gather departments such as the National Development and Reform Commission, the Ministry of Commerce, the Cyberspace Administration of China, and the State Administration of Foreign Exchange to conduct on-site verification and confirmation. The US penalty framework. CFIUS's penalty methods are also showing an escalating trend. In recent years, CFIUS enforcement has set a new record, with total fines reaching nearly $88 million and the largest single fine reaching $60 million. Before 2024, CFIUS only publicly disclosed six fines, the largest of which was $1 million. In February 2026, the U.S. Department of Justice filed its first enforcement lawsuit against the CFIUS divestiture order under the Defense Production Act. In terms of statute of limitations, the U.S. is even more aggressive than China—in January 2026, President Trump issued an executive order requiring the Chinese-controlled HieFo to divest its digital chip business acquired from Emcore in the U.S. within 180 days; the acquisition had been completed nearly two years prior. VI. Focus Areas: Commonalities and Differences Common Focus Areas. The core overlap between the U.S. and Chinese review mechanisms lies in the field of precision technology. The semiconductor supply chain is the area with the highest frequency of review and the highest rejection rate for both sides—before China halted the Manus acquisition, the US CFIUS had already effectively blocked semiconductor transactions through multiple cases. AI and related intelligent technologies are a shared priority—the Manus case marks China's first decision to prohibit investment in the AI ​​field, contrasting with the US's strict review of AI over the years. Differentiated Focuses. China's review focuses more on "technology flowing out of China" rather than "foreign investment entering China". China's regulatory pressure primarily stems from preventing the "domestic R&D, overseas cash-out" model—the loss of core technology assets is the primary concern in its reviews. In contrast, US reviews focus more on restricting foreign capital's access to US technology and incorporated geopolitical variables into their assessments earlier than China's. Furthermore, US reviews clearly integrate alliances into procedural innovation—the "fast track" process expedites the review process for investors from specific countries. VII. Global Impact: Regulatory Reflections on the Technological Race The Manus case and the release of DeepSeek V4 occurred on the same day, creating a meaningful contrast. The rise of DeepSeek V4 signifies that domestically developed AI has secured a foothold among the world's highest levels; while the halt of the Manus acquisition means that the country will not allow core AI technology assets to be lost through mergers and acquisitions. Both cases together outline two sides of China's AI strategy: on one hand, fully supporting independent innovation to grow stronger; on the other hand, safeguarding the bottom line for the security of core assets. The global impact of the Manus case is reflected in at least three levels. First, the technological rift between China and the US has deepened further, forcing multinational capital to reassess the risks of cross-border mergers and acquisitions in the AI ​​field. Second, global foreign investment regulation is expanding in a coordinated manner. From the US to the EU, from the UK to Japan, major economies are simultaneously tightening foreign investment review frameworks in key areas. Third, security reviews are replacing traditional antitrust reviews as the primary regulatory barrier to cutting-edge technology mergers and acquisitions. Looking ahead, both China and the US are expected to continue strengthening regulatory efforts in the area of ​​foreign investment security reviews. In China, the Manus case, as the first foreign acquisition in the AI ​​field to be halted, will inevitably drive further refinement of reviews regarding key technologies and data security. In the United States, CFIUS will continue to develop along the lines of differentiated treatment of investment sources, strict control of sensitive areas, and strengthening of supply chain linkages. The Omnibus Foreign Investment National Security Act, passed by Congress in December 2025, further expands the U.S. legal toolbox for technology protection. For companies engaged in cross-border transactions of cutting-edge technologies, the Manus case provides a clear compliance warning. — Proactive Pre-Notification. Chinese regulators have a zero-tolerance attitude towards "failure to report when required," and proactive disclosure is the most effective way to reduce regulatory uncertainty. All parties to the transaction should consult with the Office of the Security Review Working Mechanism to manage regulatory risks through a transparent compliance path. — Prudently Design the Transaction Structure. Treat compliance review as a red line in transaction structure design. Adopt a phased transaction arrangement, making regulatory approval a prerequisite for closing. Any design attempting to circumvent review through an offshore structure may be rejected by a thorough review. — Technology Export Compliance Management. In cross-border transfers involving key technologies, a compliance assessment should be conducted in advance against regulations such as the "Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited and Restricted from Export in China," and a technology export license should be applied for when necessary. — Data Cross-border Compliance Arrangements. Technology transfers involving the processing and export of Chinese user data must undergo legal procedures such as cross-border data security assessments. — Special Considerations for Investment in the US. Investing in the US requires not only careful consideration of CFIUS's own review process but also a thorough assessment of whether the export control systems of both countries will lead to dual compliance conflicts. A risk allocation mechanism should be proactively designed in the transaction documents. A comparison between China and the US clearly shows that China's halt to the Manus acquisition is not an isolated incident, but a typical example of the global trend of major economies tightening their scrutiny of foreign investment in cutting-edge technology sectors. While the two countries' review systems have different focuses and methods, their underlying logic is highly consistent: core technologies are essentially national strategic assets, and sovereignty over core data is inviolable. Understanding this fundamental logic is perhaps more important than familiarity with the legal provisions of both countries.

data.title: a16z: Stablecoins – A New Architecture for the Global Financial System
data.description:
Authors: Noah Levine, Guy Wuollet, Robert Hackett; Source: a16z crypto; Compiled by: Shaw, Jinse Finance

The global financial system is being restructured based on entirely new infrastructure, and the pace of this transformation far exceeds the understanding of most people outside the crypto industry.

Stablecoins are the catalyst for this transformation. They have long since evolved from niche trading tools into the underlying infrastructure of finance, and are becoming the underlying layer for a new generation of global financial products.The industry landscape map accompanying this article presents our assessment of this ongoing transformation. While specific companies within the sector may change, and industry classifications will continue to merge and evolve, the more fundamental transformation lies at the structural level: How will the new global financial technology stack gradually take shape, which sectors have matured, and what shortcomings and gaps still exist? The core logic of this transformation is that stablecoins are giving rise to a new form of Bank as a Service (BaaS). The previous wave of BaaS was essentially about fintech companies renting banking licenses and connecting to traditional, outdated core systems; this wave of transformation is fundamentally different: companies are building their businesses based on on-chain infrastructure, using self-custodied wallets to reduce transaction friction and dependence on intermediaries, while integrating basic financial capabilities such as accounts, payments, foreign exchange, and credit to create integrated end-to-end financial products. Ten years ago, achieving this full suite of financial services capabilities required holding multiple regional licenses and partnering with local banks. Now, however, any team can quickly implement these services simply by mastering this new technology stack. Stripe's acquisitions of Bridge and Privy, and Mastercard's acquisition of BVNK, clearly demonstrate that traditional financial giants are adapting to the dramatic shift in the landscape based on the same industry judgment. Industry consolidation and mergers continue, with established institutions scrambling to secure core technology stack components and gain a strategic foothold before the next generation of underlying infrastructure is finalized. All these signals indicate that the transformation of on-chain finance is irreversible. Only by proactively embracing and adapting to this trend can companies avoid being left behind.

Blockchain: Three Major Categories

The old perception that "all blockchains are vying for the same application scenarios" is crumbling. Currently, the market has differentiated into three distinct public blockchain systems, each designed based on differentiated needs and making different performance trade-offs.

... Clarifying the differences between these three types helps to understand the true direction of fintech industry implementation: **General-purpose public blockchains:** Represented by Solana, Ethereum, and their mainstream Layer 2 networks, these remain the core battleground of the crypto capital market, covering trading, lending, and decentralized finance (DeFi) businesses. This is a massive market with long-term viability, but it doesn't represent the entire industry landscape. **Payment-specific blockchains:** The second type consists of payment-specific public blockchains tailored for financial services. Networks like Stripe's Tempo and Circle's Arc compete around core capabilities that general-purpose public blockchains have never optimized: native stablecoin fees, privacy protection, and, crucially, predictable transaction costs. For fintech companies processing millions of payments daily, cost predictability is paramount. Companies investing in this sector are generally betting that dedicated public blockchains for payment scenarios will become the core settlement layer of the next-generation financial infrastructure. **Institutional Dedicated Networks:** The third type is consortium blockchains/institutional networks for compliant institutions, with Canton being a typical example. These networks balance programmability and data privacy while adhering to compliance requirements and meeting the legal risk control requirements of regulatory bodies. As banks and asset management institutions accelerate their entry, the value of this type of infrastructure will continue to increase, and its potential is gradually emerging. **Banking Sector: Key Barriers Are Loosening** For most of the past decade, the banking system has been the biggest bottleneck for crypto-native financial services. High entry barriers for partner banks and highly unstable partnerships have been the primary source of survival risk for crypto companies. While this predicament has not completely disappeared, it has been significantly improved. A group of compliant banks embracing the crypto ecosystem are proactively building bridges, connecting on-chain infrastructure with the traditional fiat currency system. The deposit and withdrawal problems that once plagued most industry players are now gradually being resolved. Smooth fiat currency channels are the core lifeline for the operation of stablecoin-native fintech companies; their value extends beyond payments, permeating the entire underlying financial chain. Stablecoin issuers: The licensing race determines the long-term landscape. The competition in the stablecoin issuance arena is unprecedented, and the competitive logic has fundamentally shifted: **regulatory compliance has become the core battleground.** Since the implementation of the US GENIUS Act, major issuers have been vying for national trust licenses from the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC). In the short term, federal government endorsement can quickly enhance compliance credibility and gain recognition from regulators and institutional partners. The long-term impact will be far-reaching: If regulators allow nationwide licensed banks to directly access the Federal Reserve's clearing network in the future, stablecoin issuers who obtain compliance licenses in advance will be deeply integrated into the core traditional financial system and become key participants in the global digital transformation of finance. The core of this competition is not brand marketing, but seizing a hierarchical position in the payment system, and more importantly, who can build the underlying foundation to support the long-term development of credit and capital markets. Liquidity service providers: the last mile problem. Stablecoins have made breakthrough progress in the mid-link of cross-border payments, namely the digital fund transfer link between different countries. Relying on stablecoins, the efficiency of cross-border settlement has been greatly improved, no longer heavily reliant on pre-deposited correspondent bank accounts, and international fund transfer frictions have been significantly reduced. The existing core weakness lies in the liquidity of stablecoins exchanged with local fiat currencies, with the problem being particularly prominent in emerging markets. The weak liquidity of most cross-border exchange channels leads to problems such as exchange rate losses, delayed settlements, and unstable quotes. If these issues are not properly addressed, they will severely restrict the large-scale adoption of stablecoins in B2B business scenarios. Currently, three main approaches are addressing this shortcoming: Foreign exchange service providers adapted to stablecoins (such as OpenFX and XFX); regional exchanges deeply rooted in the domestic fiat currency market (Bitso in Latin America, Yellowcard in Africa, and Coins.ph in Southeast Asia); and traditional banks gradually establishing themselves and directly supporting stablecoin foreign exchange settlement.

All three are indispensable: foreign exchange service providers offer technical integration capabilities, regional exchanges solidify local market liquidity, and banks provide a safety net through their balance sheets and global correspondent banking networks. No single channel can independently complete the closed loop.

Bank Integration Layer: A Low-Key Yet Indispensable Core Infrastructure

The entire stablecoin infrastructure has long been built by fintech companies, non-bank payment institutions, and crypto-native entities, independent of the traditional banking system. This model has brought efficient iteration and an open ecosystem, but it has also created structural vulnerabilities: The underlying architecture of stablecoins is inherently incompatible with the outdated core systems commonly used by traditional banks, requiring a dedicated switching layer for integration.

Bank integration services are precisely this crucial switching layer. Related companies are building dedicated infrastructure to help banks quickly launch stablecoin-related services while retaining their existing core systems, without the need for costly system-wide upgrades. Some leading pioneers have already expanded their business boundaries from crypto trading and payment settlement to areas such as on-chain lending, proactively addressing the future stablecoin ecosystem expansion needs of banks. Application Layer: Business Integration, the Birth of New Financial Infrastructure Components Two major trends are reshaping the terminal application ecosystem: The first major trend is the accelerated integration of digital banks and crypto wallets. Trading platforms are successively launching functions such as virtual accounts, payment cards, and reward programs; online digital banks are increasingly integrating crypto assets and traditional wealth management products. The boundaries between these two types of products are rapidly blurring, ultimately leading to an integrated financial terminal that serves both crypto users and general users through a unified interface. The ultimate winner in this competition may not be the company with the best current product experience, but rather a comprehensive platform that can integrate traffic, build user trust, and accurately match market demands. The second major trend is the large-scale adoption of stablecoins in enterprise banking scenarios. In regions where local dollar banking infrastructure is weak, services are unstable, or costs are extremely high (covering most of Latin America, sub-Saharan Africa, and Southeast Asia), stablecoins have enabled most businesses to achieve previously unavailable dollar-denominated business operations, encompassing scenarios such as supplier payments, global remittances, and corporate treasury management. The core of this trend is not crypto assets themselves, but the inclusive accessibility of dollar funds; the driving factor is the genuine operational needs of businesses in environments with weak local financial infrastructure and unstable systems. However, at the application layer, the more impactful and long-term core changes occur in the entire financial ecosystem beyond account services. The dollar channel is merely an entry point into the market. Whether you're a micro-enterprise owner in Lagos, a freelancer in Buenos Aires, or an ordinary saver in Jakarta, having a stable USD-denominated on-chain balance is equivalent to obtaining a ticket to a completely new financial system, granting access to a full range of financial services that were previously inaccessible: credit, investment, wealth management, insurance, and more. In emerging markets where traditional financial systems have long been lacking in service, digital banks and super applications that have taken the lead in firmly grasping user account relationships will possess an absolute advantage in cross-category sales. Payment is merely the entry point for opening an account; credit and investment businesses are the core of commercial value accumulation. The Credit Sector: A Profound Secondary Revolution If payment represents the first stage of change, then credit may become the second stage, and perhaps even more significant. The general market understanding of stablecoin growth is often limited to large-scale, narrow banking services: dollar tokenization, wallet custody, instant settlement, and on-demand redemption. However, this perspective overlooks the profound changes that will occur after the widespread adoption of stablecoins: When trillions of dollars of stablecoin funds accumulate to form a massive stock, the market will inevitably generate huge capital investment demands. Companies holding stablecoin funds will need to find ways to increase the value of their idle funds; on-chain protocols will need liquidity replenishment; and end users will eventually have borrowing needs. Therefore, a completely new on-chain lending market is almost inevitable. This is no longer the self-enclosed speculative lending model of early DeFi – using crypto assets as collateral and speculating on crypto prices; rather, it is a productive credit system that returns to the essence of banking: facilitating capital formation, lending based on real assets and accounts receivable, and providing working capital support to companies in regions lacking local banking services. The early era of DeFi's unbridled growth is coming to an end, and a more mature and sustainable on-chain financial system has arrived. This evolutionary logic is highly similar to the development of the non-standard private lending industry over the past decade. In the past, banks scaled back some of their lending business under regulatory pressure, and private lending funds filled the gap, growing from a marginal alternative asset to a core business model with a scale of trillions of dollars, directly competing with syndicated loans. The underlying logic of the on-chain lending market is the same: breaking away from the traditional banking system, relying on a new architecture to gather capital, and serving borrowers neglected by traditional finance. The core difference lies in the underlying infrastructure: on-chain finance possesses the inherent attributes of openness, programmability, and globalization, which the private lending system cannot match.
